_meta.description: Database of known ambiguous words in the Quran where the consonantal skeleton (rasm) permits multiple valid grammatical analyses. Every listed option is presented equally, allowing the user to evaluate all grammatically valid analyses independently.

categories.reading_variant: Different grammatically valid vocalizations arising from consonantal or dotting ambiguity in the rasm
categories.active_passive: The unvowelled skeleton is identical for active and passive voice
categories.form_ambiguity: Verb forms (I-X) that collapse to the same consonantal shape when vowels/shadda/hamza are removed
categories.root_ambiguity: A single surface form that could derive from two or more distinct triliteral roots
categories.case_ambiguity: The word's grammatical role (and therefore meaning) changes depending on case vowels
categories.pos_ambiguity: The same consonantal string could be a verb, noun, adjective, or particle
categories.vocalization_variant: The difference lies in the vowel pattern (not case), producing distinct lexical items from the same consonantal skeleton
categories.semantic_range: Identische Vokalisierung, rein semantische Polysemie — das Wort lässt mehrere Bedeutungen zu ohne morphologischen oder syntaktischen Unterschied.

notes: Entries marked need cross-checking against a critical apparatus.
Word positions follow the standard Uthmani word-break conventions.
All options listed are grammatically defensible analyses of the consonantal text.

options.morphology: genitive masculine singular masdar – 'dominion/rule of the Day'

entries.note: Kontext: يَوْمِ الدِّينِ (Tag des Gerichts). (1) مَالِكِ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ = 'Besitzer/Eigner des Gerichtstages' (Aktiv-Partizip, faʿil-Muster von م-ل-ك). (2) مَلِكِ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ = 'König des Gerichtstages' (Nominalform, faʿil-Muster mit Titel-Bedeutung). (3) مَلْكِ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ = 'Herrschaft des Gerichtstages' (Masdar). Alle drei Lesarten sind morphologisch und syntaktisch korrekte Genitiv-Konstruktionen (Iḍāfa).
